perturbation.original_text: It is agreed that any disputes that result between the parties whose signatures appear below shall be submitted to arbitration in accordance with The State of California Code of Procedure. The written determination of the arbitration shall be final, binding, and conclusive on the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: It is agreed that any disputes that result between the parties whose signatures appear below may be submitted to arbitration in a manner they deem appropriate. The determination of the arbitration will be considered by the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be submitted' to 'may be submitted' makes arbitration optional, contradicting the intent of mandatory arbitration. Replacing the clear 'in accordance with The State of California Code of Procedure' with 'in a manner they deem appropriate' introduces ambiguity. Replacing 'The written determination of the arbitration shall be final, binding, and conclusive on the parties' with 'The determination of the arbitration will be considered by the parties' further diminishes the binding nature of the arbitration decision.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1281.6 outlines the process for appointing an arbitrator when a method is not specified or fails, implying a structured and legally recognized arbitration process. The modified text's ambiguity regarding the method and non-binding decision contradicts this established legal framework by rendering the arbitration agreement vague and potentially unenforceable.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.6. If the arbitration agreement provides a method of appointing an arbitrator, that method shall be followed. If the arbitration agreement does not provide a method for appointing an arbitrator, the parties to the agreement who seek arbitration and against whom arbitration is sought may agree on a method of appointing an arbitrator and that method shall be followed. In the absence of an agreed method, or if the agreed method fails or for any reason cannot be followed, or when an arbitrator appointed fails to act and his or her successor has not been appointed, the court, on petition of a party to the arbitration agreement, shall appoint the arbitrator. When a petition is made to the court to appoint a neutral arbitrator, the court shall nominate five persons from lists of persons supplied jointly by the parties to the arbitration or obtained from a governmental agency concerned with arbitration or private disinterested association concerned with arbitrat
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates arbitration ('shall be submitted'), while the changed contract makes it optional ('may be submitted'). This directly contradicts the intention of ensuring a binding resolution mechanism. The original text ensures the arbitration determination is 'final, binding, and conclusive,' while the changed text only states it 'will be considered,' significantly weakening the arbitration's enforceability. The Law Explanation states the modified text renders the arbitration agreement 'vague and potentially unenforceable'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CCP 1281.6) details how arbitrators are appointed, implying a structured process. The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the method of arbitration, further diminishing its enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: I/we further agree that in consideration of that certain information of this agreement that I/we fully agree to hold all information confidential and that such information will remain the express property of THE COMPANIES.
perturbation.changed_text: I/we further agree that in consideration of that certain information of this agreement that I/we will endeavor to hold information confidential, to the best of our abilities.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'fully agree to hold all information confidential' with 'will endeavor to hold information confidential, to the best of our abilities' weakens the confidentiality obligation. 'Endeavor' and 'to the best of our abilities' introduce a subjective standard, making it difficult to prove a breach of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The original text strongly enforces confidentiality whereas the new text only requires an effort which is not enough to show trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a firm commitment to confidentiality with a weaker obligation to 'endeavor' to maintain confidentiality 'to the best of our abilities.' This introduces a subjective standard that undermines the reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy required to protect trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that CUTSA (Uniform Trade Secrets Act) requires 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' and the original text enforces confidentiality, while the changed text only requires an effort. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies California's adoption of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) under CIV 3426, which gives rise to that reasonable effort requirement.

perturbation.original_text: It is understood that this agreement is a reciprocal one between the signatories concerning the exchange of privileged information and contacts and will be held as confidential unless otherwise released or agreed as to the release only in written documentation by THE COMPANIES.
perturbation.changed_text: It is understood that this agreement is generally reciprocal between the signatories concerning the exchange of privileged information and contacts and will be treated as confidential unless released or agreed as to the release.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'will be held as confidential unless otherwise released or agreed as to the release only in written documentation by THE COMPANIES' to 'will be treated as confidential unless released or agreed as to the release' removes the 'written documentation' requirement for releasing confidential information, which means that a verbal agreement to release confidential information could be enough to waive confidentiality. This weakens the protection of confidential information by opening it to interpretations of whether something was or was not agreed upon.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Requiring written consent to release confidential information is an important aspect of protecting trade secrets, which is not followed in the modified text.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The change from requiring 'written documentation' to release confidential information to allowing release if 'released or agreed' introduces an ambiguity that weakens trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states that CUTSA (California Uniform Trade Secrets Act) 'requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 only provides the citation for the act, the Law Explanation fills in the gap by stating that written consent is an important aspect of trade secret protection. Removing the requirement of written documentation directly contradicts the reasonable effort requirement to maintain secrecy, as verbal agreements are difficult to verify and enforce, thereby undermining the protection of trade secrets, and creating a legal contradiction under the definition for Ambiguities.

perturbation.original_text: If either party sues the other party to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall, in addition to all other damages, be entitled to recover any and all legal fees incurred.
perturbation.changed_text: If either party sues the other party regarding this agreement, the prevailing party may be considered for reimbursement of some legal fees.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'to enforce any of the terms of this agreement' to 'regarding this agreement' broadens the scope of potential lawsuits covered but simultaneously changes 'shall be entitled to recover any and all legal fees incurred' to 'may be considered for reimbursement of some legal fees' introduces ambiguity and makes the recovery of legal fees discretionary rather than mandatory. 'May be considered' and 'some legal fees' are vague terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1033.5 defines allowable costs in California lawsuits, including attorney's fees when authorized by contract. The original text creates a contractual right to attorney's fees for the prevailing party. The modified text contradicts this by making the reimbursement of legal fees discretionary ('may be considered') and uncertain ('some legal fees').
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1033.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1033.5. (a) The following items are allowable as costs under Section 1032: (1) Filing, motion, and jury fees. (2) Juror food and lodging while they are kept together during trial and after the jury retires for deliberation. (3) (A) Taking, video recording, and transcribing necessary depositions, including an original and one copy of those taken by the claimant and one copy of depositions taken by the party against whom costs are allowed. (B) Fees of a certified or registered interpreter for the deposition of a party or witness who does not proficiently speak or understand the English language. (C) Travel expenses to attend depositions. (4) Service of process by a public officer, registered process server, or other means, as follows: (A) When service is by a public officer, the recoverable cost is the fee authorized by law at the time of service. (B) If service is by a process server registered pursuant to Chapter 16 (commencing with Section 22350) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code, the recoverable cost is the amount actually i
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California law regarding the recovery of legal fees. The original text stated the prevailing party 'shall...be entitled to recover any and all legal fees incurred,' establishing a clear right. The modified text changes this to 'may be considered for reimbursement of some legal fees,' making the recovery discretionary and uncertain. CCP 1033.5(a) defines allowable costs in lawsuits, which can include attorney's fees if authorized by contract. While the snippet doesn't explicitly state that attorney's fees must be mandatory if a contract allows them, the Law Explanation correctly points out the shift from a contractual right to a discretionary consideration creates a legal contradiction regarding attorney's fees.
